title: Spec-first: create or update specification before any code change
description: No implementation work may begin without a corresponding specification in the Knowledge Database. The specification documents what the system must do and why, functioning as a decision log between owner and builder.

title: Owner consent: specifications immutable without owner approval
description: Once a specification is agreed upon, it cannot be modified without explicit owner approval. This ensures the specification serves as a stable contract between owner and builder.

title: Test clarity: every test must produce unambiguous PASS/FAIL
description: Tests must have clearly defined expected outcomes. A test that cannot be definitively evaluated as PASS or FAIL is not a valid test. No partial results, no subjective interpretation.

title: Maturation: iterative spec refinement is normal, not a defect
description: Specifications mature through versions as understanding deepens. Early specs may be incomplete. The append-only versioning system preserves the full evolution of each specification.

title: Fix spec first: correct specification before changing implementation
description: When a defect is discovered, first verify whether the specification is correct. If the spec is wrong, fix the spec first. If the spec is right, fix the implementation to match.

title: Specify on contact: unspecified elements become controlled when touched
description: When implementation work touches a previously unspecified area of the system, that area must be brought under specification control before changes are made.

title: No fixes during testing: record defects as work items
description: During test execution phases, discovered defects are recorded as work items with origin 'defect', not fixed inline. Fixes happen in separate implementation sessions to maintain test integrity.

title: KB is truth: all project knowledge lives in the Knowledge Database
description: The Knowledge Database is the single source of truth for specifications, tests, work items, procedures, and documents. External files may exist for consumption but the KB is canonical.

title: Transport layer architecture decision
description: TEMPLATE: Record the decision for how components communicate (HTTP, gRPC, message queue, SSE, WebSocket). Include context (latency requirements, fan-out patterns), decision, consequences, failed approaches, and rejected alternatives.
change_reason: Architecture decision template for new projects

title: Data persistence architecture decision
description: TEMPLATE: Record the decision for primary data store (SQL, NoSQL, hybrid), partitioning strategy, consistency model, and backup/recovery approach. Include cost analysis and scaling characteristics.
change_reason: Architecture decision template for new projects

title: Identity and authentication architecture decision
description: TEMPLATE: Record the decision for user/tenant identity model (JWT, session, API key), authentication flows, token lifecycle, and multi-tenancy isolation approach.
change_reason: Architecture decision template for new projects

title: Deployment and infrastructure architecture decision
description: TEMPLATE: Record the decision for deployment target (containers, serverless, VMs), orchestration (Kubernetes, managed PaaS), CI/CD pipeline, and environment strategy (dev/staging/production).
change_reason: Architecture decision template for new projects

title: Data integrity must be maintained across all operations
description: No operation may corrupt, silently discard, or partially write data. All write operations must be atomic or compensatable. Data validation occurs at system boundaries.

title: Tenant data is fully isolated; no cross-tenant data leakage
description: In multi-tenant deployments, one tenant's data must never be visible to, modifiable by, or inferable by another tenant. Isolation is enforced at the data layer, not just the application layer.

title: Sensitive data encrypted at rest and in transit
description: All sensitive data (PII, credentials, API keys) must be encrypted at rest using AES-256-GCM or equivalent, and in transit using TLS 1.2+. Encryption keys must be managed via a dedicated key management service.

title: All public API endpoints must include version prefix
description: Public-facing API endpoints must use a version prefix (e.g., /api/v1/). Breaking changes require a new version. Previous versions remain supported for at least one release cycle.

title: Database schema changes must be backward compatible
description: Schema migrations must be additive (new fields, new collections) rather than destructive (renamed fields, removed columns). This enables zero-downtime deployments where old and new code coexist.

title: Deployments must not cause service interruption
description: All deployments must use rolling updates or blue-green strategies that maintain service availability throughout the deployment process. Health checks gate traffic shifting.

title: Authentication: all API endpoints require valid credentials
description: Every API endpoint (except /health, /ready, and /api/status) must require authentication. Supported methods: API key (header), JWT bearer token, or session cookie. Unauthenticated requests return HTTP 401.

title: Authorization: role-based access control for all operations
description: Operations must be gated by role (owner, admin, member, viewer) and tenant scope. A user in tenant A must not be able to perform operations on tenant B's resources regardless of their role.

title: Multi-tenancy: system supports multiple isolated tenants
description: The system must support multiple tenants with isolated data, configuration, and usage tracking. Tenant provisioning, activation, deactivation, and deletion must be supported. Each tenant receives a unique widget key for client-side integration.

title: Health endpoints: /health (liveness) and /ready (readiness)
description: The system must expose /health (returns 200 if process is running) and /ready (returns 200 with structured JSON if all dependencies are reachable, 503 otherwise). Used by container orchestrator probes and deployment scripts.

title: Structured logging: all components emit JSON-formatted logs
description: All application components must emit structured JSON logs with fields: timestamp, level, message, component, tenant_id (when applicable), correlation_id. Log levels: DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL.

title: Error handling: consistent error response format across all endpoints
description: All API errors must return a consistent JSON structure: {error: string, code: string, details: object|null, request_id: string}. Internal errors must not leak stack traces or implementation details to clients.

title: Rate limiting: per-tenant request throttling with configurable limits
description: The system must enforce per-tenant rate limits to prevent abuse and ensure fair resource allocation. Limits must be configurable per tier. Rate-limited requests return HTTP 429 with Retry-After header.
